threats.title: _claim for Non-ETH Tokens Sweeps All Contract ETH via Absolute Balance Instead of Swap Delta
threats.hypothesis_description: In `_claim` for non-ETH tokens, after the swap via `_fillQuote`, `claimedAmount` is set to `address(this).balance` (absolute balance), not the delta from the swap. Any pre-existing ETH in the contract—from `receive()`, selfdestruct force-sends, or prior swap residuals—is captured by the claimer and deposited into lpETH on their behalf. The `_fillQuote` function correctly computes the delta (`boughtETHAmount = address(this).balance - boughtETHAmount`) but only uses it for the event. The actual claim amount ignores this delta entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code comment 'At this point there should not be any ETH in the contract' acknowledges the assumption, but the `receive()` function accepts ETH from anyone at any time, directly violating this assumption. This is a fundamental accounting error where the protocol conflates the swap output with unrelated ETH.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimedAmount = address(this).balance; lpETH.deposit{value: claimedAmount}(_receiver); — uses absolute balance not swap delta

preconditions: Claim phase active (startClaimDate set by convertAllETH)
User has non-zero balance for an LRT token (balances[user][token] > 0)
Contract holds non-zero ETH from any source (receive(), selfdestruct, prior residuals)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: absolute balance used instead of delta
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _fillQuote computes delta correctly in boughtETHAmount but it's only used for event emission; _claim uses address(this).balance capturing all ETH
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No pre-swap balance snapshot; no delta calculation for claimedAmount

analysis_questions: What are all sources of ETH that can land in the contract post-conversion?
Can the 0x swap callback or lpETH.deposit callback send additional ETH back?
Is there a realistic scenario where significant ETH accumulates?
validation_requirements: Send ETH to contract via receive() after convertAllETH
Call claim with valid LRT and observe claimedAmount exceeds swap output
Verify the surplus lpETH goes to the claimer

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After convertAllETH, ETH arrives via receive() (accidental send, selfdestruct, or even a failed swap from another protocol). Any LRT holder calling claim() captures this ETH as bonus lpETH. The claimer receives their swap output PLUS all residual ETH.
prerequisites: Non-zero ETH in contract from any source

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All ETH sent to the contract post-conversion is stolen by next LRT claimer
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Anyone who accidentally sends ETH, or ETH sent via selfdestruct
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — lpETH is minted and sent to claimer atomically

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for _claim behavior when contract holds pre-existing ETH

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal — just need 1 wei of LRT locked pre-activation
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain: claimedAmount in Claimed event exceeds expected swap output

threats.title: UniswapV3 Calldata Validation Bypass via Non-Standard ABI Encoding Offset
threats.hypothesis_description: _decodeUniswapV3Data reads encodedPath data from hardcoded calldata offsets, skipping the dynamic bytes offset pointer at position 4. Standard ABI encoding places the bytes data at offset 0x80 (from args start), which the decoder assumes. An attacker can craft non-standard ABI encoding with a different offset pointer: fake path data at the standard position passes validation (correct inputToken=LRT, outputToken=WETH), while the real path data at the actual offset is followed by the 0x exchange proxy. This allows the attacker to route swaps through arbitrary intermediate pools.
threats.investigation_rationale: The decoder explicitly skips the ABI bytes offset (line 'p := add(p, 36)' jumps over selector + offset). The 0x proxy uses standard ABI decoding and follows the offset pointer. This discrepancy creates a TOCTOU-like gap between what's validated and what's executed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: p := add(p, 36) skips bytes offset pointer; encodedPathLength/inputToken/outputToken read from hardcoded positions, not from ABI-decoded offset

preconditions: Attacker has LRT balance in the contract
Claim phase is active
0x exchange proxy accepts non-standard ABI encoded calldata and follows offset pointer

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Hardcoded offset assumption for dynamic bytes parameter
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: encodedPathLength read from add(p, 96) = offset 132 assumes standard layout; inputToken from add(p, 128) and outputToken from add(p, encodedPathLength+108) read fake data
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No verification that the offset pointer at position 4 equals 0x80; no comparison between decoded path and ABI-standard-decoded path

analysis_questions: Does the 0x exchange proxy's UniswapV3 handler follow the ABI offset pointer for the bytes parameter?
Can crafted calldata with offset != 0x80 still execute a valid swap with different token path?
What concrete exploitation is possible given that sellAmount and recipient ARE at fixed offsets?
validation_requirements: Craft calldata with bytes offset = 0x100, fake path at 0x80 position, real different path at 0x100
Verify _validateData passes while exchange proxy uses the real path
Test if swap through attacker-controlled pool extracts value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker crafts UniswapV3 calldata with non-standard ABI layout. Fake encodedPath at standard offset: [LRT][fee][WETH] passes validation. Real encodedPath at actual offset: [LRT][fee][AttackerPoolToken][fee][WETH] routes through attacker's manipulated pool, extracting value from the swap via poor exchange rate while appearing to be a standard LRT->WETH swap.
prerequisites: Ability to deploy a Uniswap V3 pool with manipulated liquidity

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Attacker extracts value from own swap via routing manipulation; could also bypass outputToken check to receive non-WETH tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Primarily the attacker's own funds, but could enable further exploits if combined with other vectors

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for non-standard ABI encoding of calldata

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Standard swap gas + pool creation costs
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Swap event shows different path than validated

threats.title: TransformERC20 Recipient Validation Bypassed Due to Uninitialized Variable Defaulting to Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: In `_validateData`, the `recipient` variable is only assigned for UniswapV3 exchange type. For TransformERC20, `_decodeTransformERC20Data` does not return a recipient. The local variable `recipient` defaults to `address(0)`. The final check `if (recipient != address(this) && recipient != address(0))` passes because `address(0) != address(0)` is false. This means TransformERC20 swaps have NO recipient validation at all. The unchecked `transformations` array in TransformERC20 calldata could contain arbitrary operations that redirect funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code structure shows recipient is validated for UniswapV3 but the same check for TransformERC20 is silently bypassed via uninitialized variable. The transformations array within the TransformERC20 calldata is never parsed or validated, creating an arbitrary execution vector through the trusted exchange proxy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: TransformERC20 branch does not set `recipient`; default address(0) passes the recipient != address(0) check at line 322-324

preconditions: Attacker has LRT balance
Claim phase active
0x TransformERC20 supports transformations that can redirect output or execute arbitrary calls

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Uninitialized recipient variable defaults to address(0), passing the explicit check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: recipient validated only for UniswapV3; TransformERC20 transformations array completely unchecked
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No recipient decoding for TransformERC20; no validation of transformations content

analysis_questions: Does 0x TransformERC20 always send output to msg.sender, or can transformations redirect output?
What transformation types are available and can they execute arbitrary calls?
Can a crafted transformation send tokens to an attacker address before the final output settlement?
validation_requirements: Verify recipient defaults to address(0) for TransformERC20 path
Test crafted transformations that redirect intermediate tokens
Verify whether arbitrary sub-calls via transformations can exploit approved tokens

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker crafts TransformERC20 calldata with valid inputToken/outputToken/amount but includes transformations that redirect the swap output or intermediate tokens. Since recipient is never validated and transformations are unchecked, the exchange proxy executes the full transformation pipeline, potentially sending funds to attacker-controlled addresses or executing malicious callbacks.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Attacker could redirect swap output or manipulate intermediate steps; combined with residual approvals could drain other users' tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All LRT depositors if contract-held tokens are drained
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None if tokens are transferred out atomically

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for TransformERC20 with crafted transformations

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Complex — transformations execute within exchange proxy

threats.title: Residual ERC20 Approval to Exchange Proxy Persists After Swap, Enabling Token Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: _fillQuote approves `exchangeProxy` for `_amount` of the sell token but never resets the approval to 0 after the swap. If the exchange proxy doesn't consume the full allowance (partial fill scenarios, or specific 0x transformer behavior), the residual approval persists. Since the exchange proxy is a publicly callable contract, an attacker could potentially use a crafted call to the exchange proxy to consume the leftover approval and transfer tokens out of PrelaunchPoints.
threats.investigation_rationale: The standard pattern for interacting with swap routers is to approve, swap, then reset approval to 0. _fillQuote only approves and swaps. Any unconsumed allowance remains indefinitely. Combined with the TransformERC20 validation bypass (TECH-H004), this creates a compounding risk.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)); — approval set but never reset to 0 after swap

preconditions: A prior claim has been made for a specific LRT token
The exchange proxy didn't consume the full approval (partial fill, 0-amount swap, or specific transformer behavior)
The exchange proxy allows external callers to use the allowance (via transferFrom or specific functions)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Approve before swap with no reset after
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If 0x swap partially fills, remaining allowance persists; 0x proxy is publicly callable
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No approve(exchangeProxy, 0) after swap; no check that allowance is fully consumed

analysis_questions: Under what conditions does the 0x proxy NOT consume the full approved amount?
Can an external caller use the 0x proxy to trigger transferFrom on PrelaunchPoints' approved tokens?
Does the next _fillQuote call for the same token overwrite or add to the approval?
validation_requirements: Execute a claim that leaves residual approval on the exchange proxy
Attempt to use the 0x proxy as external caller to consume the residual
Verify token transfer from PrelaunchPoints to attacker

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User A claims token X. _fillQuote approves 100 X to exchange proxy. Swap consumes 95 X (partial fill). 5 X approval remains. Attacker crafts a call to the 0x exchange proxy that does transferFrom(PrelaunchPoints, attacker, 5) using the residual approval. Attacker steals 5 X tokens belonging to other users.
prerequisites: Partial fill or 0-amount swap leaving residual approval
0x proxy supports operations consuming third-party allowances

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of LRT tokens from the contract proportional to residual approvals
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the same LRT token with unclaimed balances

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test checking approval state after _fillQuote

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None beyond gas for the exploit call
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time after a claim with residual approval

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Cause Balance Inflation Leading to Last-Claimer Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: In `_processLock`, `balances[_receiver][_token] += _amount` credits the full requested amount, but for fee-on-transfer tokens, `safeTransferFrom` delivers fewer tokens than `_amount`. The contract's actual token balance becomes less than the sum of user balances. When the last users try to claim or withdraw, the contract lacks sufficient tokens, causing reverts.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract comment explicitly states 'ONLY add wrapped LRT tokens. Contract not compatible with rebase tokens' but does NOT mention fee-on-transfer incompatibility. The `allowToken` function can whitelist any ERC20 at the owner's discretion. If a fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted, all users of that token face insolvency risk.
deployment_assumptions: Owner must whitelist a fee-on-transfer token via allowToken()
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script whitelists tokens (allowedTokens array is empty in script). Token whitelisting happens post-deployment via allowToken(). No evidence preventing fee-on-transfer token addition.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(_token).safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), _amount); then balances[_receiver][_token] += _amount — no balance delta check

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted via allowToken()
Users deposit that token
Multiple users try to withdraw or claim the same token

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Balance credited without checking actual received amount
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: balances[_receiver][_token] += _amount uses requested amount, not actual received
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No pre/post transfer balance check; no documentation of fee-on-transfer exclusion in code

analysis_questions: Are any of the target LRT tokens (weETH, ezETH, rsETH, rswETH, uniETH, pufETH) fee-on-transfer?
Could any of these tokens add fee-on-transfer behavior via upgrade?
What happens when last user's withdrawal or claim reverts?
validation_requirements: Deploy with a mock fee-on-transfer token
Have multiple users deposit
Verify last user's withdrawal reverts due to insufficient balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner whitelists a token with 1% transfer fee. Users deposit 100 tokens each. Contract receives 99 per deposit but credits 100. When later users withdraw, safeTransfer reverts for last users whose withdrawal exceeds the remaining contract balance.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Last depositors lose their tokens entirely; loss proportional to fee percentage * total deposits
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Late claimers/withdrawers of the affected token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partial — early users can withdraw, late users cannot

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing sum of balances vs contract token balance

threats.title: _fillQuote Uses Raw approve() Instead of SafeERC20.safeApprove, Causing DoS for Non-Standard Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: _fillQuote calls `require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount))` using raw IERC20.approve instead of SafeERC20.safeApprove. In Solidity 0.8, if the token's approve() doesn't return a value (like USDT), the ABI decoder reverts when trying to decode a bool from empty return data. This would permanently block all claims for that token, as _fillQuote is the only path for LRT claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract correctly uses SafeERC20 for `safeTransfer` and `safeTransferFrom` throughout, but inconsistently uses raw `approve` in _fillQuote. This code defect breaks the SafeERC20 usage pattern and creates a silent DoS for any non-standard token.
deployment_assumptions: An allowed token must not return bool on approve()
threats.config_evidence: Target LRTs (weETH, ezETH, rsETH, etc.) are standard ERC20 and return bool. However, allowToken() can whitelist arbitrary tokens post-deployment without validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)); — raw approve, not safeApprove; inconsistent with SafeERC20 usage elsewhere

preconditions: A token that doesn't return bool on approve() is whitelisted
User has locked that token and tries to claim post-conversion

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Raw approve() in require() instead of safeApprove
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Solidity 0.8 ABI decoder reverts if approve() returns empty bytes instead of bool

analysis_questions: Do any current or planned LRT tokens not return bool on approve()?
What happens to user funds if claim permanently reverts? Can they withdraw in emergency?
Is the token's approve non-standard but safeTransferFrom works?
validation_requirements: Test _fillQuote with a mock token that doesn't return bool on approve
Confirm revert occurs in require statement
Verify emergency withdrawal is the only recovery path

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner whitelists a token whose approve() doesn't return data. Users lock the token. After conversion, all claim() calls for this token revert at _fillQuote's require(approve()) line. Users can only recover via emergency mode withdrawal of the raw LRT (not lpETH).

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All locked amounts of the affected token cannot be converted to lpETH
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors of the affected token
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Emergency mode withdrawal returns raw tokens (not lpETH)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test with non-standard ERC20 approve behavior

threats.title: convertAllETH Captures Pre-existing lpETH via balanceOf, Inflating Claim Ratio
threats.hypothesis_description: In convertAllETH, `totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(address(this))` reads the TOTAL lpETH balance, not just the amount received from the deposit. If lpETH tokens are sent to the contract before conversion (by anyone), totalLpETH is inflated. The proportional claim formula `userStake * totalLpETH / totalSupply` distributes the excess lpETH to all ETH depositors. The donor loses their lpETH to benefit all depositors proportionally.
threats.investigation_rationale: While not directly profitable for an attacker (the donor loses), this breaks the 1:1 conversion invariant stated in the documentation. It could be used for manipulation if combined with a large ETH deposit: attacker deposits ETH, donates lpETH to inflate ratio, claims more than 1:1.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(address(this)); — captures total balance including pre-existing lpETH, not deposit delta

preconditions: lpETH tokens sent to contract before convertAllETH is called
lpETH contract address is known before conversion (from setLoopAddresses)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Should use pre-deposit balance snapshot: totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(this) - preLpETHBalance

analysis_questions: Can an attacker profit by depositing ETH + donating lpETH before conversion?
What is the net economic effect of the inflation on the attacker vs other depositors?
How much lpETH can the 7-day window allow to accumulate?
validation_requirements: Send lpETH to contract before convertAllETH
Verify totalLpETH includes the donated amount
Verify individual claims exceed 1:1 ratio

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After setLoopAddresses reveals lpETH address, attacker acquires lpETH (from secondary market or direct deposit) and sends it to PrelaunchPoints. When owner calls convertAllETH, totalLpETH includes both the deposit output and the donated lpETH. All ETH depositors receive inflated claims. The donor bears the cost.
prerequisites: Ability to acquire and send lpETH to contract

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Breaks 1:1 conversion invariant; economic impact depends on donation size relative to totalSupply
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All ETH depositors receive inflated claims; lpETH donor loses

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for pre-existing lpETH before conversion

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing totalLpETH vs expected from ETH deposit

threats.title: Unrestricted ownership transfer to address(0) can permanently brick admin-only recovery and lifecycle actions
threats.hypothesis_description: setOwner() allows setting owner to address(0) (or an otherwise unreachable address). This would permanently disable onlyAuthorized functions (setLoopAddresses, convertAllETH, allowToken, setEmergencyMode, recoverERC20), potentially trapping user funds (especially if loop is not activated or if a later emergency requires emergencyMode).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect (missing validation) that can cause irreversible protocol DoS without needing external dependencies to fail. It also magnifies impact of other issues (e.g., inability to respond to 0x/lpETH problems).

proof_of_signal.pattern: owner = _owner; with no nonzero or sanity check; onlyAuthorized gates future admin actions

preconditions: Current owner calls setOwner(address(0)) or to an address that cannot transact
Users still have locked balances and require admin actions for lifecycle progression or emergency response

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: admin role can be burned unintentionally
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: permanent loss of ability to call onlyAuthorized functions
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no require(_owner != address(0)) and no 2-step ownership transfer

analysis_questions: Are there any off-chain/operational constraints preventing setting owner to zero?
Is there any alternative recovery mechanism if owner is lost?
What user fund states become unrecoverable without admin (pre-activation vs post-activation)?
validation_requirements: Simulate setOwner(address(0)) then attempt all onlyAuthorized functions
Model fund liveness for ETH and LRT depositors across phases without admin

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If owner key is compromised or owner makes an operational mistake, attacker/owner sets owner=address(0), permanently disabling emergencyMode/activation/recovery, trapping funds and preventing conversion/withdrawal coordination.
prerequisites: Ability to submit a transaction as current owner (mistake or key compromise)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential permanent lock of all user funds held in the contract; inability to recover accidental ERC20s; inability to activate conversion
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None on-chain if owner becomes unreachable

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests indicated for ownership transfer edge cases (zero address, lost owner) or admin-liveness invariants

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: on-chain owner set to 0 is easily observable; impact is irreversible

threats.title: Dust/grief deposits via lockETHFor can trap victims in unexpected claim/withdraw flows and create operational DoS for claim processing
threats.hypothesis_description: Any user can call lockETHFor(_for) to create ETH balances for arbitrary victims. While victims can later withdraw/claim, forced deposits can create unwanted bookkeeping entries and potentially hinder a victim's preferred actions (e.g., forcing them to manage extra token positions, or forcing them into claim flows if UI assumes no positions). In addition, dust ETH balances across many victims can create event spam and operational load for off-chain points/referral processing.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an explicit, permissionless capability. While not necessarily a direct fund loss, it can be used for targeted griefing and operational disruption of the points system (which is a core protocol asset), and may have secondary effects if UI/backends assume deposits are opt-in only.

proof_of_signal.pattern: lockETHFor allows msg.sender to credit balances[_for][ETH] with msg.value without _for consent

preconditions: Deposits are still allowed (block.timestamp < loopActivation)
Attacker can send small msg.value repeatedly

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: griefing via dust deposits and event spam; potential victim UX/accounting disruption
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no allowlist for callers or recipient opt-in; no minimum deposit amount

analysis_questions: Does the backend/points system treat 'Locked' events as authoritative for points, potentially enabling referral farming or victim attribution manipulation?
Are there any per-user/per-token assumptions in frontend/backend that break when unsolicited deposits exist?
Is there a realistic cost-effective spam threshold (gas + msg.value) that could overwhelm indexing/ops?
validation_requirements: Run adversarial simulations: mass lockETHFor dust to many recipients and observe backend/indexer behavior
Verify whether points attribution can be manipulated (referral bytes32) even without victim interaction

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker dust-deposits ETH for many addresses (or a targeted victim) with arbitrary referral codes, generating misleading attribution in off-chain points tracking and forcing victims to manage unexpected balances.
prerequisites: Sufficient ETH to fund dust amounts and gas

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Limited direct on-chain loss; potential off-chain rewards/points integrity impact
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Victims can withdraw/claim, but reputational/ops damage may persist

threats.test_coverage_gap: No explicit anti-spam/min-deposit tests or backend assumptions tests

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: estimate per-spam cost vs feasible spam volume
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: spikes in Locked events and many small balances are observable

threats.title: Unvalidated TransformERC20 Transformations Enable Arbitrary Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: When using the `TransformERC20` exchange option, `_validateData` only verifies the `selector`, `inputToken`, `outputToken`, and `inputTokenAmount`. It completely ignores the `transformations` array in the 0x calldata. The 0x `transformERC20` function executes these transformations, which can include arbitrary contract calls. If the contract has any residual token approvals to the 0x proxy, an attacker can craft a transformation to steal those tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Forwarding unvalidated complex payloads to a proxy that executes arbitrary logic is highly dangerous, especially when the proxy holds approvals from the calling contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only validates selector, inputToken, outputToken, and amount. Ignores transformations array.

preconditions: Contract has reached startClaimDate
Contract has residual token approvals to the 0x exchangeProxy (e.g., from previous partial fills)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The transformations array is not parsed or restricted, allowing arbitrary execution within the 0x proxy context
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Validation of the transformations array or strict whitelisting of allowed transformation types

analysis_questions: What specific transformations are available in the deployed 0x proxy?
Can a transformation execute a `transferFrom` using the PrelaunchPoints contract's residual allowance?
validation_requirements: Identify a 0x transformation that allows arbitrary calls or token transfers
Craft a payload using that transformation to transfer tokens from PrelaunchPoints to the attacker
Execute the payload via `claim`

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) A previous legitimate claim leaves a residual allowance of TokenA to the 0x proxy. 2) Attacker calls claim with a tiny amount of TokenB using TransformERC20. 3) The calldata includes a transformation instructing the 0x proxy to call `TokenA.transferFrom(PrelaunchPoints, attacker, residualAmount)`. 4) Validation passes because TokenB details are correct. 5) 0x proxy executes the transformation, stealing TokenA.
prerequisites: Residual token allowances to the 0x proxy

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of any tokens with residual allowances to the 0x proxy
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users whose tokens are stolen from the contract balance

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for malicious TransformERC20 transformations

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via unexpected Transfer events originating from the 0x proxy

threats.title: Unprotected self-transfer of lpETH may permanently break ETH-claimer payouts (lpETH.safeTransfer to address(this))
threats.hypothesis_description: In the ETH claim path, a caller can set _receiver to address(this) (via claimAndStake calling _claim with address(this)). If lpETH is a non-standard ERC20 that reverts on self-transfer (or implements ERC777-style hooks that revert), claimAndStake(ETH,...) could be bricked, or even plain claim(ETH,...) could be impacted if receiver is ever set to this contract in other flows. This creates a liveness/fund-access risk for ETH depositors relying on claim/claimAndStake after conversion.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code assumes lpETH behaves like a standard ERC20 and that safeTransfer to the contract itself is always safe. Many ERC20s are fine, but non-standard tokens can revert on self-transfers or have transfer restrictions. Because ETH withdrawals are blocked after startClaimDate (even in emergency mode), any lpETH transfer failure is a hard lock for ETH depositors.
deployment_assumptions: Owner-configured lpETH token is non-standard (e.g., reverts on transfer(to=self) or on transfer to certain addresses), or implements hooks that can revert under some conditions
Users use claimAndStake(ETH,...) (which routes receiver=address(this)) or any future path uses receiver=this
threats.config_evidence: lpETH address is owner-supplied via setLoopAddresses(); no on-chain interface enforcement that lpETH is a vanilla ERC20.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimAndStake() calls _claim(..., address(this), ...) causing lpETH.safeTransfer(_receiver, claimedAmount) with _receiver == address(this) on ETH path

preconditions: startClaimDate has been set (convertAllETH executed) so claim paths are the only redemption for ETH depositors
User has a non-zero balances[user][ETH]
lpETH.safeTransfer(address(this), amount) reverts for the configured lpETH implementation OR triggers a revert via hook/restriction

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Assumes lpETH transfer-to-self is always safe; no fallback redemption route
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If lpETH transfer fails, ETH depositors cannot withdraw due to UseClaimInstead gating post startClaimDate
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No alternative redemption, no try/catch around lpETH transfer, no emergency ETH-unwind post conversion

analysis_questions: Does lpETH allow transfer(to=self) and transfer(to=this contract) without restrictions?
Does lpETH implement ERC777 hooks or other callbacks that can revert under normal circumstances?
Would claim() (ETH path) ever use _receiver == address(this) besides claimAndStake?
validation_requirements: Integrate against the real lpETH implementation and run claimAndStake(ETH) end-to-end
Fuzz receiver behavior by mocking lpETH to revert on self-transfer and confirm depositor lock scenario
Confirm whether any operational runbook relies on claimAndStake for ETH depositors

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If lpETH is configured to revert on self-transfer, any ETH depositor using claimAndStake (or any flow that sets receiver=this) will be unable to claim/stake. Since withdraw(ETH) is blocked after startClaimDate regardless of emergencyMode, funds become effectively locked.
prerequisites: Owner configured a problematic lpETH implementation OR lpETH later changes behavior (e.g., upgradeable token)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential permanent lock of ETH depositor redemption path (cannot claim/stake; cannot withdraw ETH post-conversion)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: ETH/WETH depositors attempting claimAndStake; potentially all ETH depositors if lpETH transfers are broadly restricted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low without contract upgrade/migration; recoverERC20 blocks lpETH recovery

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for claimAndStake(ETH) against real lpETH behavior; no negative tests for lpETH transfer restrictions

threats.title: Zero-Cost Infinite Drain of Contract ETH via Percentage Truncation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `claim` function calculates `userClaim = userStake * _percentage / 100`. For small `userStake` values or small percentages, this rounds down to 0. A 0-amount swap executes successfully, but `claimedAmount` is set to `address(this).balance` rather than the swap delta. The attacker's balance is reduced by 0, allowing them to repeatedly mint `lpETH` equal to the contract's entire ETH balance without spending any tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: This arithmetic truncation combined with absolute balance accounting allows an attacker to infinitely capture any residual ETH in the contract (from `receive()`, `selfdestruct`, or other users' swaps) and mint unbacked `lpETH`, severely diluting the `lpETH` pool and stealing protocol value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: userClaim rounds to 0, claimedAmount = address(this).balance

preconditions: Contract has > 0 native ETH balance after `convertAllETH` (e.g., via `receive()`)
Attacker has a non-zero balance of any allowed LRT token

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker executes 0-amount claims to repeatedly capture the contract's absolute ETH balance as minted lpETH
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains lpETH equal to contract ETH balance per transaction, at zero token cost

economic_questions: How much ETH typically accumulates in the contract post-conversion?
Can the attacker force ETH into the contract to amplify the attack?
validation_requirements: Test `claim` with `_percentage` that results in `userClaim == 0`
Verify 0x proxy accepts 0-amount swaps without reverting
Confirm `balances[msg.sender][_token]` remains unchanged

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (dust amount of LRT to pass `userStake == 0` check)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Standard gas costs for claim (~150k gas)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of any ETH residing in the contract, repeatable infinitely

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after any ETH is sent to the contract post-conversion

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol, ETH senders, and legitimate lpETH holders
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All residual ETH in the contract + infinite dilution of lpETH

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify `claimedAmount` captures full balance instead of swap delta

threats.title: Emergency Mode Fails to Protect ETH Depositors Post-Conversion: Funds Permanently Locked if lpETH is Broken
threats.hypothesis_description: After convertAllETH sets startClaimDate, the withdraw function contains a hard check `if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead()` that executes REGARDLESS of emergencyMode. ETH depositors are forced to use claim(), which requires a functioning lpETH contract. If lpETH is buggy, malicious, or paused, ETH depositors have zero recovery path: withdraw reverts with UseClaimInstead, claim reverts because lpETH.safeTransfer fails, and recoverERC20 blocks lpETH. Emergency mode, designed as the safety valve, cannot rescue ETH depositors post-conversion.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a state machine design flaw where the emergency escape hatch is rendered non-functional precisely when it's most needed. The UseClaimInstead check at lines 281-283 is outside the emergency mode conditional, creating an irrecoverable dead-end state for ETH depositors.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() checks emergencyMode bypass for time restrictions but UseClaimInstead check at line 281 executes unconditionally after balance zeroing, blocking ETH withdrawal post-startClaimDate regardless of emergencyMode

preconditions: convertAllETH has been called (startClaimDate set to current timestamp)
lpETH contract becomes non-functional (reverts on transfer, is paused, or is malicious)
Users have non-zero ETH balances

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Emergency mode bypasses time checks but not the UseClaimInstead revert
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner sets malicious lpETH via setLoopAddresses, waits for TIMELOCK, calls convertAllETH. All ETH sent to malicious lpETH. Emergency mode cannot rescue.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All locked ETH (potentially millions) permanently inaccessible to depositors

economic_questions: What is the total ETH locked when convertAllETH is called?
Are there any alternative recovery paths beyond withdraw and claim?
Can the owner deploy a new contract to recover funds?
validation_requirements: Trace all code paths available to ETH depositors after convertAllETH + emergencyMode
Verify that recoverERC20 blocks lpETH recovery
Confirm no fallback mechanism exists for broken lpETH scenarios

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: None (design flaw, not an active attack)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of all locked ETH permanently inaccessible
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - denial of service / permanent fund lock
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent once convertAllETH executes with broken lpETH

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total loss of deposited ETH for all ETH depositors

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Map all state transitions and verify dead-end state existence
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm UseClaimInstead is outside emergencyMode conditional

threats.title: Immutable exchangeProxy Cannot Be Updated If 0x Proxy Is Upgraded Maliciously, Risking All LRT Approvals
threats.hypothesis_description: The exchangeProxy (0xDef1C0ded9bec7F1a1670819833240f027b25EfF) is set as immutable in the constructor and is the 0x TransparentUpgradeableProxy. During every LRT claim, _fillQuote approves this proxy for the sell token amount. The 0x proxy is upgradeable by 0x governance. If 0x governance is compromised or acts maliciously, a new implementation could steal all approved tokens. Since exchangeProxy is immutable, PrelaunchPoints cannot switch to a different swap router. Additionally, _fillQuote never resets approvals after swaps, so partial fills leave residual approvals that persist across transactions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The combination of immutable external dependency on an upgradeable proxy, persistent token approvals, and no ability to change the exchange creates a systemic risk. The approval-not-reset pattern (INV-STATE-007) compounds this by leaving dangling approvals.
threats.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.s.sol line 10: EXCHANGE_PROXY = 0xDef1C0ded9bec7F1a1670819833240f027b25EfF (0x Exchange Proxy, a TransparentUpgradeableProxy on Ethereum mainnet)

proof_of_signal.pattern: _fillQuote approves exchangeProxy for _amount but never resets to 0 after swap; exchangeProxy is immutable and points to upgradeable 0x proxy

preconditions: 0x Exchange Proxy is upgraded with malicious implementation (external compromise)
Users have locked LRT tokens and are in claim phase
Any prior _fillQuote call left residual approvals (partial fills)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)) sets approval but no approval reset to 0 after swap
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised 0x proxy implementation calls transferFrom to drain approved tokens from contract
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All LRT tokens held by contract at risk of theft

economic_questions: What is the total value of LRT tokens held by the contract during claim phase?
Does the 0x proxy typically consume full approval in standard swaps?
What are the 0x governance controls and upgrade timelock?
validation_requirements: Check if standard 0x swaps consume full approval (no residual)
Verify no approval reset after _fillQuote completion
Assess 0x proxy upgrade governance and timelock

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All LRT tokens held by the contract

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When maximum LRT tokens are locked and unclaimed
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total loss of all unclaimed LRT tokens

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All LRT depositors who haven't claimed yet
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total value of all locked LRT tokens

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify residual approval state after typical 0x swap
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test partial fill scenarios and resulting approval state

threats.title: lock() for Allowed Token That Is Also lpETH Address Creates Shared Balance Pool Conflict
threats.hypothesis_description: If owner calls allowToken(address(lpETH)) after setLoopAddresses, users can lock lpETH as an LRT token via lock(). During claim, _fillQuote approves the exchange proxy for lpETH and swaps it to ETH. Since the contract holds lpETH for BOTH ETH claimers (from convertAllETH) and LRT depositors (from lock(lpETH)) in the same ERC20 balance, the exchange proxy's transferFrom pulls from the shared pool. While mathematically the user's locked lpETH amount should equal what's pulled, any swap slippage or partial fill means the contract's lpETH balance could drop below what's needed for remaining ETH claimers. The allowToken function has no check against the lpETH address.
threats.investigation_rationale: allowToken has no validation that _token != address(lpETH). Since lpETH is set after construction via setLoopAddresses, constructor-time checks are impossible. If owner allows lpETH, the shared balance creates a potential for ETH claimer insolvency through swap mechanics.
deployment_assumptions: Owner must call allowToken(address(lpETH)) after setLoopAddresses
threats.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.sol: allowToken has no check against lpETH address. lpETH is set at runtime. No deployment evidence of this being prevented.

proof_of_signal.pattern: allowToken(address _token) sets isTokenAllowed[_token] = true with no check that _token != address(lpETH)

preconditions: Owner calls allowToken(address(lpETH)) after setLoopAddresses
Users lock lpETH as an LRT token before loopActivation
Users claim lpETH-as-LRT during claim phase, pulling from shared lpETH pool

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No namespace separation between lpETH held for ETH claimers and lpETH locked as LRT token; both in same ERC20 balance
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Lock lpETH as LRT → claim swaps lpETH from shared pool → if swap returns less ETH than lpETH sent, remaining ETH claimers face insolvency
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts value from ETH claimer pool via unfavorable lpETH→ETH swap rates

economic_questions: Is there any deployment check that prevents allowToken(lpETH)?
Would the swap route for lpETH→ETH create favorable/unfavorable rates?
Can the shared pool actually be depleted below ETH claimer needs?
validation_requirements: Verify allowToken accepts lpETH address
Model shared lpETH pool depletion under various swap scenarios
Test whether claim(lpETH_addr, 100, ...) successfully pulls from shared balance

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on lpETH/ETH swap rate differential
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable - depends on swap execution quality

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After convertAllETH but before most ETH users claim
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could drain shared lpETH pool creating insolvency

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: ETH depositors whose lpETH claims fail due to insufficient balance
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% loss for last ETH claimers
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Proportional to volume of lpETH locked as LRT

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if allowToken(lpETH) is callable and what the result is

threats.title: Persistent DoS of LRT Claims via Dusting
threats.hypothesis_description: Attackers can persistently DoS user claims by dusting their accounts via lockFor, causing strict calldata amount validation to fail.
threats.investigation_rationale: The claim function strictly validates that the 0x calldata inputTokenAmount exactly matches the calculated userClaim (based on userStake). Because anyone can increase a victim's userStake using lockFor, an attacker can invalidate the victim's pre-signed/pre-calculated 0x calldata by dusting their account.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (inputTokenAmount != _amount) { revert WrongDataAmount(inputTokenAmount); }

preconditions: Victim is attempting to claim an LRT token
loopActivation has not passed (deposits still open)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Strict equality check on a value derived from a manipulable state variable

operational_questions: Can an attacker reliably dust a victim before their claim transaction is confirmed?
Does the strict equality check prevent the claim from executing?
validation_requirements: Simulate a victim generating 0x calldata for their current balance
Execute lockFor with 1 wei to the victim
Attempt to execute the victim's claim transaction and verify it reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for users preparing to claim LRTs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Periodically call lockFor with 1 wei of the target LRT to the victim's address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim's userStake increases, altering the calculated userClaim
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim's claim transaction reverts due to WrongDataAmount

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users are unable to claim their LRTs, causing frustration and potential loss of favorable swap rates
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - victim must recalculate and submit quickly

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Frequent lockFor calls with dust amounts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: High rate of reverted claim transactions with WrongDataAmount

threats.title: Owner Sets Malicious lpETH/Vault to Steal All Deposited ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: setLoopAddresses allows the owner to set arbitrary lpETH and lpETHVault addresses with no validation. A malicious or compromised owner can set lpETH to a contract that accepts ETH deposits but returns nothing or traps funds. After the 7-day TIMELOCK, convertAllETH sends ALL contract ETH to the malicious lpETH. Users who don't withdraw during the grace period lose all funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Single-owner model with no multisig, no governance vote, no address validation. The 7-day grace period is the only protection, but users must actively monitor. The addresses are permanent once set (loopActivation prevents re-calling setLoopAddresses).
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script PrelaunchPoints.s.sol shows owner is set from PRIVATE_KEY with no multisig wrapper

proof_of_signal.pattern: lpETH = ILpETH(_loopAddress); lpETHVault = ILpETHVault(_vaultAddress); no address validation, no multisig, permanent once set

preconditions: Owner key is compromised or owner is malicious
setLoopAddresses has not been called yet (before 120-day deadline)
Users don't withdraw during 7-day grace period

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: convertAllETH sends 100% of address(this).balance to owner-specified lpETH contract
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious lpETH contract accepts ETH but doesn't return lpETH tokens, or returns worthless tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker steals entire totalSupply worth of ETH

economic_questions: What is the total value at risk (totalSupply at deployment)?
How effective is the 7-day grace period as a protection mechanism?
Can the owner change addresses to malicious ones after initially setting legitimate ones?
validation_requirements: Confirm setLoopAddresses can only be called once
Verify 7-day TIMELOCK enforcement
Check if there's any way to reset addresses after initial setting

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant ETH deposits, before user awareness

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All ETH depositors who don't withdraw during grace period

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify single-call restriction and TIMELOCK enforcement

threats.title: No Token Disallow Mechanism - Compromised Whitelisted Tokens Cannot Be Removed
threats.hypothesis_description: The allowToken function enables whitelisting tokens but there is no corresponding disallowToken function. Once a token is allowed via isTokenAllowed[token] = true, it cannot be removed. If a whitelisted LRT token is discovered to be malicious, compromised, or upgraded to have harmful behavior (fee-on-transfer, rebase, blacklisting), users can continue depositing it during the deposit phase, potentially corrupting contract accounting.
threats.investigation_rationale: The isTokenAllowed mapping can only be set to true, never back to false. The admin has no mechanism to revoke token whitelist status.

proof_of_signal.pattern: isTokenAllowed[_token] = true; no corresponding function to set isTokenAllowed[_token] = false

preconditions: A whitelisted token becomes compromised or changes behavior
Deposit phase is still active (before loopActivation)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: One-way whitelist; no removal function exists in the contract
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner has no mechanism to fix even if they detect the issue
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During deposit phase for the compromised token

operational_questions: Can any target LRT tokens be upgraded by their protocol to add harmful behavior?
Would the only mitigation be to call setLoopAddresses early to close deposits?
Does emergency mode provide sufficient fallback?
validation_requirements: Verify no disallowToken function exists
Check if setLoopAddresses can be used as emergency deposit halt
Assess upgradability of target LRT tokens

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identified token becomes compromised or adds transfer fee
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users continue depositing compromised token (no disallow mechanism)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Contract accounting corrupted by non-standard token behavior
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Late withdrawers/claimers cannot recover full balance

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Continued deposits of harmful tokens during deposit phase
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Emergency mode can enable raw token withdrawal
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Deposits and claims for affected token

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unusual token behavior changes for whitelisted tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large deposits of a token after behavior change

threats.title: setLoopAddresses Accepts Zero Addresses and Is Irreversible - Potential Permanent Contract Bricking
threats.hypothesis_description: setLoopAddresses has no zero-address validation for _loopAddress or _vaultAddress. It sets loopActivation = block.timestamp, which prevents re-calling (onlyBeforeDate(loopActivation) fails in same or later block). If the owner accidentally sets address(0) for lpETH, convertAllETH will attempt lpETH.deposit{value: totalBalance}(address(this)) on address(0), which will revert. Users can only withdraw during the grace period. If that window passes, funds are locked until emergency mode is enabled.
threats.investigation_rationale: No input validation on critical address parameters in a one-time-call function. The loopActivation = block.timestamp at line 309 prevents correction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: lpETH = ILpETH(_loopAddress); no require(_loopAddress != address(0)); loopActivation = uint32(block.timestamp) prevents re-calling

preconditions: Owner calls setLoopAddresses with address(0) for _loopAddress or _vaultAddress
No mechanism to correct after the call

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No address(0) check; loopActivation reset prevents re-calling; one-shot function

operational_questions: Can emergency mode withdrawal recover all funds if lpETH is address(0)?
Would ETH depositors still be blocked by UseClaimInstead after convertAllETH fails?
Is there any other recovery path?
validation_requirements: Test setLoopAddresses with address(0)
Test convertAllETH with lpETH = address(0)
Verify emergency mode is sufficient fallback

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner accidentally (or maliciously) sets lpETH to address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: convertAllETH attempts to call address(0).deposit{value:...} - reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Grace period expires; users who didn't withdraw are stuck
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Only recovery: owner enables emergencyMode for non-ETH withdrawals; ETH withdrawals work pre-startClaimDate

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Conversion blocked; deposits locked until emergency withdrawal
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Recoverable via emergency mode (for both ETH and non-ETH before startClaimDate)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: lpETH or lpETHVault set to address(0)

threats.title: Emergency mode fails to provide an ETH depositor escape hatch after conversion, enabling permanent lock if lpETH integration fails
threats.hypothesis_description: Even when `emergencyMode` is enabled, `withdraw(ETH)` still reverts after `startClaimDate` due to `UseClaimInstead()`. If `lpETH` becomes non-functional/malicious or claim transfers revert, ETH depositors cannot withdraw and cannot recover via recoverERC20 (lpETH is blocked), creating a dead-end state where funds are stuck.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a state-machine defect with severe operational consequences during an incident involving lpETH. While it requires admin misconfiguration or external dependency failure (low realism under strict threat model), it is precisely the scenario emergency mode is intended to cover per documentation.
deployment_assumptions: Owner sets lpETH to a contract that later fails/reverts on transfer/deposit, or lpETH becomes non-functional
convertAllETH has already been executed (startClaimDate set)
Users are ETH/WETH depositors (balances[user][ETH] > 0) attempting to exit
threats.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.sol withdraw() contains UseClaimInstead check not gated by emergencyMode; setLoopAddresses has no validation of lpETH/vault addresses; documentation claims emergency mode is a failsafe.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw(): if (_token == ETH) { if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead(); } executed regardless of emergencyMode

preconditions: convertAllETH executed (startClaimDate set)
lpETH transfer or deposit path fails/reverts for claimants
User has balances[user][ETH] > 0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Emergency mode is documented as a failsafe, but ETH withdrawals are still blocked post-claim-date.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Yes (lpETH address selection and conversion timing).

operational_questions: What is the incident response plan if lpETH breaks after convertAllETH?
Is there any off-chain or contract-level mechanism to unwind lpETH back to ETH for users?
Do monitoring and staged rollout procedures exist before executing convertAllETH?
validation_requirements: Test scenario: set lpETH to a contract that reverts on transfer; run convertAllETH; enable emergencyMode; verify users cannot withdraw ETH and cannot claim.
Confirm recoverERC20 cannot recover lpETH to users/owner.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Trigger dependency failure condition (lpETH malfunction) after conversion.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users attempt withdraw(ETH) in emergency mode and fail; claims fail as well, locking funds.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None for attacker; impact arises from dependency failure/misconfig.
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total lock of ETH depositor funds; severe incident requiring migration or social recovery.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Very high—may require new contract and off-chain compensation.

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Triggered by an lpETH failure after conversion.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Claim tx reverts from lpETH token calls after startClaimDate.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Emergency mode enabled but ETH withdrawals still reverting.

threats.title: Deployment script deploys with empty allowedTokens list: protocol usability and withdrawal/claim liveness may depend on later allowToken calls
threats.hypothesis_description: The provided Foundry script deploys PrelaunchPoints with an empty allowedTokens array. Only WETH is auto-allowed in the constructor. If production deployment repeats this pattern unintentionally, all intended LRT deposits will revert (TokenNotAllowed), causing protocol launch failure or user lock-in to ETH/WETH only.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a realistic configuration gap: scripts are often used in production. While not an on-chain exploit by an attacker, misconfiguration can materially disrupt protocol operations and user expectations.
deployment_assumptions: Production uses PrelaunchPoints.s.sol as-is or similarly passes an empty allowedTokens array
Owner does not call allowToken for intended LRTs before users attempt to lock them
threats.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.s.sol defines address[] public allowedTokens; never populated before new PrelaunchPoints(EXCHANGE_PROXY, WETH, allowedTokens).

proof_of_signal.pattern: address[] public allowedTokens; // never set; passed into constructor

preconditions: Deployment uses script/config that passes empty allowedTokens
Users attempt to lock LRT tokens before owner allowlists them

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: LRT deposits revert unexpectedly; could break points program and user flows
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No constructor requirement that allowedTokens length > 0; no events confirming initial allowlist set

analysis_questions: Is there a separate production deployment process that populates allowedTokens?
Is allowToken intended to be called immediately post-deploy?
Do docs/UI assume specific LRTs are depositable from block 1?
validation_requirements: Review actual deployment pipeline and parameters for mainnet
Check if allowToken transactions are planned/automated before launch
Add deployment-time checks or post-deploy verification scripts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not attacker-driven: users attempting to lock intended LRTs will revert, potentially causing loss of user trust and broken program timelines until owner corrects via allowToken.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct loss, but major operational disruption; users may miss points accrual window
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High (owner can allowToken), but requires timely admin action

threats.test_coverage_gap: No deployment verification tests ensuring allowlist contains expected tokens

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediate: TokenNotAllowed reverts for all non-WETH tokens

threats.title: setOwner Enables Instant Ownership Transfer Without Two-Step Confirmation or Timelock
threats.hypothesis_description: The setOwner function immediately transfers ownership to any address with no two-step confirmation, no timelock, and no validation that _owner != address(0). If the owner key is compromised, an attacker can instantly take full control: set malicious lpETH/vault addresses (if before activation), toggle emergency mode, whitelist arbitrary tokens, call convertAllETH with malicious lpETH, or transfer ownership to prevent recovery. The single-step transfer also means accidental mistyped addresses permanently lock out the owner role.
threats.investigation_rationale: While key compromise is an off-chain event (LOW realism), the lack of a two-step pattern or timelock means there is zero recovery window. Combined with the owner's extensive powers (set lpETH, toggle emergency, convert ETH), instant ownership transfer amplifies the impact of any key compromise to a protocol-ending event.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setOwner(address _owner) immediately sets owner = _owner with no pending/acceptance step, no timelock, no zero-address check

preconditions: Owner private key is compromised (off-chain event)
OR owner accidentally calls setOwner with wrong address

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised key → setOwner(attacker) → setLoopAddresses(malicious) → convertAllETH → all ETH stolen
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: 100% of locked ETH + control of protocol

economic_questions: What is the total value at risk under owner control?
Is there any external governance or multisig wrapping the owner address?
What attack sequence maximizes extraction after ownership takeover?
validation_requirements: Verify no external timelock or multisig on owner address
Map maximum damage sequence: setOwner → setLoopAddresses → convertAllETH
Confirm no recovery mechanism exists after ownership transfer

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for setOwner + setLoopAddresses + convertAllETH sequence
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of all locked ETH and LRT tokens
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: 3 transactions (potentially single block if before activation)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Before setLoopAddresses has been called (maximum control)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if owner is EOA or multisig in production deployment
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Map full attack sequence from ownership takeover to fund extraction

threats.title: Irreversible Protocol Brick via Unvalidated Loop Addresses
threats.hypothesis_description: Lack of address validation in setLoopAddresses allows the owner to permanently brick the protocol, locking all ETH.
threats.investigation_rationale: The setLoopAddresses function can only be called once because it resets loopActivation to block.timestamp. It lacks zero-address or contract-code checks. If the owner makes a mistake, the addresses are permanently set to invalid values, causing convertAllETH to revert and permanently locking all ETH deposits.
threats.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.sol: setLoopAddresses function lacks validation and is a one-time call.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No address(0) checks; loopActivation = uint32(block.timestamp) prevents re-calling

preconditions: Owner calls setLoopAddresses with incorrect or zero addresses

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: One-time critical configuration function lacks basic input validation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Protocol survival depends entirely on flawless admin execution

operational_questions: Is there any recovery mechanism if setLoopAddresses is called incorrectly?
Does emergencyMode allow ETH recovery if convertAllETH cannot be called?
validation_requirements: Call setLoopAddresses with address(0)
Attempt to call convertAllETH and verify it reverts
Verify ETH depositors cannot withdraw

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner accidentally or maliciously calls setLoopAddresses with invalid addresses
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: loopActivation is updated, preventing any correction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: TIMELOCK expires, owner attempts to call convertAllETH but it reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol is permanently bricked; ETH cannot be converted or withdrawn

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Admin loses ability to activate protocol
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total loss of all locked ETH

threats.title: Owner can set lpETH/lpETHVault to address(0) or incompatible contracts, permanently bricking conversion/claims due to one-shot configuration
threats.hypothesis_description: setLoopAddresses has no zero-address or interface sanity checks, and can only be called once (loopActivation set to block.timestamp makes onlyBeforeDate(loopActivation) fail thereafter). If the owner sets incorrect addresses (including address(0) or non-conforming contracts), convertAllETH/claimAndStake may revert and the system cannot be corrected through another setLoopAddresses call.
threats.investigation_rationale: Although owner action is required (low realism under the on-chain-only test), this is an operationally common failure mode. Because the configuration is effectively immutable after setLoopAddresses, a single mistake can cause long-lived downtime and/or lockups.
deployment_assumptions: Owner address is controlled by an operator who could misconfigure or use an incorrect address source during launch
No external operational circuit breaker exists (e.g., governance-controlled migration) beyond setEmergencyMode
threats.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.s.sol deploys with empty allowedTokens and does not call setLoopAddresses; no scripted validation of lpETH/lpETHVault addresses is shown.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setLoopAddresses assigns lpETH/lpETHVault without nonzero checks; sets loopActivation = uint32(block.timestamp) making it effectively one-time

preconditions: Owner calls setLoopAddresses with an incorrect lpETH and/or lpETHVault address (including address(0) or incompatible implementation)
Users have deposited and are relying on conversion/claim paths

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: One-shot address wiring with no validation and no ability to repair after call
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner must configure correctly; no secondary admin safeguards in code
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: At activation time; error becomes permanent immediately after setLoopAddresses

operational_questions: What checks are performed off-chain before calling setLoopAddresses (bytecode allowlist, interface probing, chainid verification)?
Is there an emergency migration plan if lpETH/vault addresses are wrong?
Are there runbooks preventing address(0) or testnet/mainnet mixups?
validation_requirements: Test setLoopAddresses with address(0)/wrong contract then attempt convertAllETH, claimAndStake, claim; confirm failure modes
Confirm inability to correct addresses afterwards due to modifier gate

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Trigger misconfiguration (owner mistake or incorrect address source)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: System enters grace period with permanently wired bad addresses
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Conversion/claim operations revert; users face liveness failure and rely on withdrawals only
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If emergency limitations apply (see OPS-H004), some users may be locked

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Conversion and/or staking claims may be impossible, potentially forcing mass withdrawals or causing lockups
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High; no in-contract remediation beyond emergencyMode and withdrawals (which have limitations)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setLoopAddresses emits LoopAddressesUpdated to suspicious/zero addresses
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: EmergencyMode toggled soon after activation due to failures

threats.title: Emergency mode does not allow ETH withdrawal after conversion, enabling permanent lock if lpETH is broken
threats.hypothesis_description: withdraw(ETH) contains an unconditional post-check that reverts when block.timestamp >= startClaimDate, even if emergencyMode is enabled. After convertAllETH sets startClaimDate, ETH depositors can only claim lpETH. If lpETH.safeTransfer or lpETH.deposit becomes non-functional (or lpETH was misconfigured), ETH depositors can be permanently locked without an emergency escape hatch.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete state-machine defect: emergency mode is documented as a failsafe but fails to provide ETH recovery post-conversion. This is operationally relevant because integration failures (lpETH bugs, paused tokens, blacklists) can occur during incidents.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw(): if (_token == ETH) { if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead(); } is outside the emergencyMode gating

preconditions: convertAllETH has executed (startClaimDate set)
lpETH transfer/deposit path is impaired (buggy lpETH, paused, blacklist, revert on transfer, etc.)
Users have balances[user][ETH] > 0 to recover

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Emergency mode bypasses timing checks but ETH branch still blocks withdrawals after claim date
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Incident may require owner action to toggle emergencyMode, but it does not help ETH depositors post-conversion
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after convertAllETH if lpETH becomes non-functional

operational_questions: What are the failure modes of lpETH (pausable/blacklist/upgradability) and how likely are they in production?
Is there an off-chain compensating control (manual lpETH redemption/airdrop) if claims fail?
Do runbooks prevent conversion until lpETH is proven stable and audited?
validation_requirements: Simulate lpETH reverting on transfer; confirm claim fails
Toggle emergencyMode and confirm withdraw(ETH) still reverts post startClaimDate

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: System converts ETH to lpETH and opens claims
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: lpETH becomes non-functional (integration incident or misconfiguration)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users cannot claim lpETH and cannot withdraw ETH even under emergencyMode
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None for users; incident triggered by external dependency failure

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol-wide inability for ETH depositors to exit; high severity incident
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High; may require new contract deployment and off-chain remediation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Emergency response and user fund recovery

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low once lpETH fails; systemic impact

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Spike in failed claim txs; emergencyMode toggled but withdraw(ETH) still failing
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: lpETH contract upgrade/pause events correlated with claim failures
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users unable to exit despite emergency announcements
